question.label: Self-read opener
question.name: Before I share my view, how do you think the last stretch has gone?
question.description: Anchors the conversation in their self-assessment first; gap vs manager's view becomes a signal.

answer: I can name wins, but I am not always sure they match the team bar.
note: He can identify wins but explicitly says he is unsure they match the team bar, which is a strong clarity gap; the fact he can name wins suggests some engagement.

question.label: Team bar mismatch
question.name: You said 'team bar' — what feels unclear about it right now?
question.description: Drills into the specific mismatch he named so the conversation stays anchored in his words.

answer: Feedback has helped, though I still miss consistency under time pressure.
note: He says feedback helps but consistency breaks under time pressure, which signals a mild remaining clarity gap around applying the bar in real conditions.

question.label: Time pressure consistency
question.name: Under time pressure, what part slips first for you?
question.description: Drills into the specific inconsistency he named so the evidence stays concrete.

answer: I sometimes focus on polish and lose sight of the core message.
note: He names a specific clarity failure under pressure: prioritising polish over the core message.

question.label: Quality bar
question.name: When you say a brand design is good, what tells you it's good?
question.description: Surfaces Carlos's current internal quality standard and whether it is concrete enough to guide decisions.

answer: I want clearer examples of what excellent junior-level work looks like here.
note: He names a specific absence — concrete examples of excellent junior-level work — which is a strong clarity gap and mild growth constraint.

question.label: Excellent examples
question.name: What examples of excellent junior-level work would make the bar clearer for you?
question.description: Drills into the specific examples he says are missing so the quality bar becomes concrete.

answer: When reviews are broad, I am not sure what to change first.
note: He describes broad reviews leaving him unsure what to change first — a strong clarity gap, with mild strain from not knowing where to focus; wellbeing has 0 touches after 3+ turns, so next question must include it.

question.label: Expectation gap
question.name: Where do my expectations for your work feel different from yours right now?
question.description: Names alignment gaps without assuming the answer, and makes the mismatch discussable.

answer: I would like to grow faster in concept rationale, not just execution speed.
note: He names a mismatch in emphasis — wanting faster growth in concept rationale over execution speed — which is a strong expectations-clarity gap and mild positive engagement signal; also reveals a growth thread worth following.

question.label: Concept rationale
question.name: Concept rationale — what does stronger work there look like to you?
question.description: Drills into the specific growth area he named so the gap becomes concrete and discussable.

answer: A tighter loop on one skill at a time would help me improve.
note: Names a missing feedback structure — a tighter loop on one skill at a time — which is a strong clarity deficit; [THREAD-DEFERRED-WINDDOWN] tighter loop on one skill at a time.

question.label: Growth blocker
question.name: What is getting in the way of the next skill you want to build?
question.description: Surfaces the real constraint on near-term development, whether it's skill, context, or habit.

answer: If we align on concrete criteria, I can work against them confidently.
note: He names missing alignment on concrete criteria as the blocker, which is a strong growth constraint and mild clarity deficit.

question.label: Two-week focus
question.name: What's the first concrete change you want to make before we meet again?
question.description: Closes on a specific commitment with a near-term behavioral change.

note: [SKIP] no signal — planner bypassed, queue carried forward
